question: how to make home garden
title: How to Make a Home Garden That Actually Works (From Someone Growing in Houston)
metaDescription: Want to know how to make a home garden that feeds you real, living food? Scotty walks you through the whole thing — from picking your spot to building soil that grows the kind of calories that matter.
content:
# How to Make a Home Garden That Actually Works (From Someone Growing in Houston)

I want to tell y'all something real quick before we get into the how-to part. Every home garden guide on the internet tells you to start small, pick your vegetables, buy some soil, and water regularly. That's not bad advice. But it's also missing the whole point.

The point of a home garden — at least the way I think about it — isn't just to grow food. It's to grow *living* food. Food that has the bacteria and the prokaryotes and the whole biological web still intact when you eat it. That's something your grocery store fundamentally cannot give you. The supply chain kills it. The refrigeration delays it. By the time that tomato sits in a clamshell on a shelf, the living element is long gone.

So yeah, we're going to talk about raised beds and sunlight and watering. But we're also going to talk about why this actually matters. Because if you understand the *why*, the *how* gets a whole lot more motivating.

## Step One: Pick a Spot That Gets Real Sun

This is the step that trips up more people than any other. You need a minimum of six hours of direct sunlight a day for most vegetables. That's not six hours of bright shade. That's direct sun hitting the leaves and soil.

In Houston, we've got sun in abundance from April through October. That's a blessing and a challenge. The challenge is that the Texas summer heat can be brutal, and some crops like lettuce bolt when it gets above 85 degrees. But the blessing is that our growing season is genuinely long — you can get two full growing seasons in most years, one in spring and one in fall.

Walk your yard at different times of day and watch the shadows. Mark where the sun lands. Don't assume — actually look. The best spot is often not where you think it is.

Also consider proximity to water. You want your garden close enough to a hose that watering doesn't feel like a punishment. I finally put in a rainwater collection system for my backyard beds, and that changed everything. Rainwater is alive in a way that treated municipal water isn't. It doesn't have chlorine that'll suppress your soil biology.

## Step Two: Build Raised Beds (And Do It Simple)

For a first home garden, raised beds are the move. They give you control over your soil mix, they drain better than in-ground beds in Houston's clay-heavy soil, and they warm up faster in spring. I've built raised beds out of simple two-by-fours cut in half. You don't need cedar, you don't need composite boards, you don't need anything fancy.

The University of Maryland Extension recommends beds no wider than four feet so you can reach the center from either side without stepping on the soil. Stepping in your beds compacts the soil, and compaction is the enemy of root growth and soil biology. Keep your paths outside the beds. Keep your feet out of the growing space.

A 4x8 foot bed is a classic starting size. That's 32 square feet of growing space. That's enough to grow lettuce, herbs, some tomatoes, and cucumbers in the same season. Start with one or two beds. Once you see how they work and how much time you're actually spending, you can expand.

For depth, aim for at least 12 inches of growing media. Roots need room. Eighteen inches is better if you can swing it.

## Step Three: Build Soil, Not Just Fill It

Here's where most beginner guides fall completely flat. They tell you to buy a bag of potting mix from the hardware store and fill your beds. That stuff is fine as a starting point, but it's usually biologically dead. It's peat or coir with some perlite and maybe a slow-release synthetic fertilizer pellet. That's not living soil.

Living soil is built over time with organic matter. Compost is the foundation. I'm talking real compost — finished, dark, earthy-smelling stuff that was once food scraps and yard waste and maybe some manure. Compost feeds the bacteria and fungi that feed your plants. Albert Howard's whole life work came down to one truth: the fertility of the soil depends on the diversity and activity of the organisms within it.

For a new raised bed, I'd suggest a mix of roughly one-third compost, one-third garden soil or topsoil, and one-third something to open it up like aged wood chips or perlite. Then every season, top it with another inch or two of compost and let the earthworms and soil organisms pull it down. Never till if you can help it. Tilling destroys fungal networks that your plants depend on.

The Old Farmer's Almanac recommends a soil test before planting so you know your pH and basic nutrient levels. That's genuinely useful, especially in Houston where our soils can swing alkaline. Most vegetables prefer a pH between 6.0 and 7.0.

## Step Four: Start With Crops That Want to Grow

This sounds simple but it saves a lot of heartache. Some vegetables are forgiving and productive even when you're still figuring things out. Some vegetables will defeat you at every turn until you've built up your soil and your skills.

For Houston beginners, here's what I'd plant first:

**Fall/Winter (September–February):** Kale, collards, Swiss chard, lettuce, spinach, arugula, radishes, green onions. These are the crops that practically grow themselves in our mild winters. Lettuce in particular goes absolutely wild in Houston from October through February.

**Spring (March–May):** Cherry tomatoes, cucumbers, green beans, squash, herbs like basil and cilantro. These want warmth and sun, and Houston's spring delivers.

Stay away from broccoli and cauliflower until you've got a season or two under your belt. They're finicky about timing. Same with melons unless you've got space to let them sprawl.

NC State Extension's guide on home vegetable gardening points out what experienced gardeners know: zucchini, radishes, and cherry tomatoes are among the most reliable crops for beginners. Plant what wants to grow where you are.

## Step Five: Water Smart, Not More

Most vegetables want about an inch of water per week. In Houston's summer, you might need more — the heat just evaporates water faster than you'd expect. But overwatering is just as damaging as underwatering for most crops. Soggy roots mean root rot, and root rot means dead plants.

The trick is mulch. A two-to-three inch layer of wood chip mulch on the surface of your beds does something remarkable. It slows evaporation, keeps the soil temperature stable, and as it decomposes it feeds your soil biology. Mulch is one of the best things you can do for a home garden, full stop.

I water in the morning when I can. Morning watering lets foliage dry during the day, which reduces fungal disease pressure. Evening watering leaves leaves wet overnight, which is an invitation for problems.

Drip irrigation or soaker hoses are the most efficient delivery systems. They put water right at the root zone and keep foliage dry. If you're hand-watering, aim for the base of the plant, not the leaves.

## The Big Picture: You're Building a Living System

Here's what I want y'all to walk away with. A home garden is not a series of isolated transactions — buy seed, buy soil, grow vegetable, eat vegetable. It's a living system that gets better every year when you treat it right.

The soil you build this year will be richer next year. The earthworms you attract will multiply. The beneficial insects you stop poisoning will colonize your beds and manage your pest pressure. The compost you make from your kitchen scraps will come back to your garden as fertility, completing the decay cycle that Howard described and that nature has been running since long before we showed up.

Gabe Brown says the five principles of soil health — minimize disturbance, keep soil covered, maintain living roots, maximize diversity, and integrate livestock — apply at any scale. Even a couple of 4x8 raised beds in a Houston backyard can be managed by those principles. Stop tilling. Keep something growing. Add organic matter constantly. Build the biology.

This is the difference between a garden that limps along and a garden that feeds you. The biology does the work. Your job is to feed and protect it.

---

## Sources

- The Old Farmer's Almanac. "How to Start a Vegetable Garden (Beginner's Guide)." https://www.almanac.com/vegetable-gardening-for-beginners
- University of Maryland Extension. "How to Start a Vegetable Garden." https://extension.umd.edu/resource/how-start-vegetable-garden
- NC State Extension. "Vegetable Gardening: A Beginner's Guide." https://content.ces.ncsu.edu/home-vegetable-gardening-a-quick-reference-guide
- University of Minnesota Extension. "Planting the vegetable garden." https://extension.umn.edu/planting-and-growing-guides/planting-vegetable-garden
- National Agricultural Library, USDA. "Vegetable Gardening." https://www.nal.usda.gov/plant-production-gardening/vegetable-gardening
